figures.caption: Fig. 3: Overview of our CTPCM, which is divided into local prototype construction and global prototype construction.
Fig. 4: Visualization of the attention map for the data-driven method Hyrsm [7] and our FSAR-LLaVA on HMDB51. We also provide the Video-LLaVA’s QA results for these videos using “Unknown” prompts.
Fig. 6: Performance comparison between our knowledge-driven FSAR-LLaVA and the datadriven baseline of the 5-way 1-shot task on five widely used datasets. All methods share the same vision encoder.

Fig. 1: (a), (b), and (c) indicate the pipeline of the data-driven unimodal/multimodal method, knowledge-driven CapFSAR, and our FSARLLaVA, respectively. Our FSAR-LLaVAUnknown, which uses the fixed input prompt: “What’s the action of the video?” without introducing additional textual label information, fully leverages the multimodal features of MLLM and achieves state-of-the-art performance that requires minimal parameters, as depicted in part (d), which refers to the performance comparison in the HMDB51 5-way 1-shot task.
Table 1: Comparison under 5-way k-shot settings on five widely-used benchmarks including HMDB51, UCF101, Kinetics, SSv2-Small, and SSv2-Full.
Table 12: Performance comparison of the hidden layer features from Video-LLaVA. The boldfacen and underline font indicate the highest and the second highest results. Note that “Avg.” denotes Average.

Fig. 5: Direct qualitative analysis of the Video-LLaVA’s output with different types of prompts. For clarity, we highlight words in blue to represent accurate action labels, in red to reveal the question posed in Video-LLaVA, and in green to indicate inaccurate action descriptions.

Fig. 2: Overview of our FSAR-LLaVA: Visual inputs and text prompts are processed through our knowledge base to extract multimodal tokens Tm from the multimodal decoder’s hidden layer. These tokens are downsampled and decoupled into visual tokens Tv and textual tokens Tt. The tokens are then passed through the Multimodal Feature-enhanced Module, and features from both branches are processed in the Composite Task-oriented Prototype Construction Module to obtain the enhanced support set class prototypes P̃S v and P̃S t . Finally, the class prototypes and query features are fed into the Multimodal Matching Metric to yield the probability distribution pQ2S and loss LQ2S .

Table 8: Complexity analysis for 5-way 1-shot HMDB51 evaluation. Our FSAR-LLaVA utilizes the “Unknown” prompts here. “Time” represents the training time cost of each 5-way 1-shot task. “Acc” means the 5-way 1-shot accuracy on HMDB51. All experiments are carried out on one Nvidia H20 GPU.
